essential_learning_outcomes.elo_description: Plan and carry out fair tests in which variables are controlled and failure points are considered to identify aspects of a model or prototype that can be improved.

specific_curriculum_outcomes.description: Define the terms: Technology, Volts, Potential, Electrical Circuit, Electric Current (electrons), Direct Current (DC), Alternating Current (AC), Electric Switch, Conductor

Define the terms: Insulator, Simple Machine, Lever, Mechanical Advantage, Effort Force, Class of Lever, Inclined Plane, Wheel, Spring Balance, Ideal Mechanical Advantage, Wedge, Screw, Hinge, Slot/Philips/Roberson/Allen, Stress, Geodesic Dome, Collar and Rafter Ties, Struts
Demonstrate with a picture that they understand the difference between first-, second- and third-class levers.
Compare electrical circuits that will generate light and those that won’t.
Give examples of devices that operate by alternating current.
Give examples of devices that operate by direct current.
Account for how a switch controls whether a device is able to operates by dc/ac.
Explain with a diagram how a lever can solve a problem.
Distinguish between insulator and conductor using examples.
Give examples of common devices that make use of insulating materials.
Identify and describe simple mechanical devices (hinges, screws, screw drivers) (ST 2 PS FMS 4)
Realise the importance of safety in handling tools an making gadgets. (ST 2 TE NT 3)
Formulate problems and do research in development of technological devices. e.g. construct models and gadgets. (ST 3 TE TM 1)
Identify and explain the ways in which strengthening mechanisms are used to resist stress in structures under load. E.g. struts to resists compression, ties to resists tension. (ST 4 PS FMS 7)
Identify and describe how forces can alter shapes of objects. E.g. bending, stretching, pulling, twisting. (ST 4 PS FMS 8)
Explain how ties can be used in roof construction to mitigate the effects of strong winds.
Identify and name parts of a simple circuit and demonstrate by setting one up. (ST 5 PS EN 1)
Distinguish giving examples, the differences between conductors and insulators. (ST 5 PS EN 2)
Name the instrument used to measure force and the unit in which it is measured. (ST 5 PS FMS 1)
Identify a number of common levers and describe how they operate to make work easier. (ST 5 PS FMS 3)
Name the points of a lever giving some common examples. (ST 5 PS FMS 4)
Identify the main parts of a wheel and an axle giving examples and describing how the objects function. (ST 5 PS FMS 5)
Define a simple machine, giving examples, and explaining how they make work easier. (ST 5 PS FMS 6)
Infer that an incline plane decreases the force required to lift an object. (ST 5 PS FMS 8)
Account for how a wedge is simply an example of an inclined plane and list examples of its use, explaining how they work. (ST 5 PS FMS 9/ ST 6 PS FMS 11)
Observe a picture and identify human inventions (door example)
Use pictures and classroom materials to build simple circuits and explain how they operate to provide a lit bulb.
Classify items which operate by direct current or alternating current.
By experiment, distinguish conductors and insulators.
By experiment, distinguish the effort force required for various configurations of the first-class lever.
Measure the force to pull and object up an inclined plane using a spring balance.
Measure the force to lift an object using a spring balance.
Calculate the ideal mechanical advantage (IMA) of an incline plane.
Describe the process by which a hinge can be properly installed on a door.
Use drawings to explain how ties can be used in constructing a roof to resist high winds.
Perform calculations on effort force and load for a range of first-class lever.
Calculate ideal mechanical advantage for a wedge.
Evaluate, design and build a prototype wind car.
Use Fablab software to create three dimensional geometric figures that may assist with the design of stronger structures.
Having measured force acting on an object using a spring balance, design and use a simple device used to measure force. (ST 5 PS FMS 2)
Predict the force that will balance a lever with an off-centre fulcrum. (ST 6 PS FMS 4)
Research the use of a wheel in a variety of situations, citing their impact. (ST 6 PS FMS 5)
Examine the use of simple machines in simple devices in the home and community. E.g. openers, egg beaters, seesaws (ST 6 PS FMS 6)
Identify and appreciate that the gadgets, tools and structures used in their homes and communities are made by humans. (ST 3 TE UT 1)
Demonstrate interest/curiosity to design a better wind car.
Demonstrate a spirit of inquiry as learners perform experiments.
Demonstrate an appreciation for past cultures who exercised human ingenuity/inventiveness to gain mechanical advantage over enormous tasks.
Demonstrate a spirit of collaboration & cooperation as learners worked together on experiments.
Demonstrate an appreciation of the importance of safety as we construct things in our classroom and in the greater world.
When conducting practical and group work, display sensitivity and offer assistance to peers who may have physical or learning challenges.
Participate actively in classroom discussions.

inclusive_assessment_strategies: Practising Vocabulary: Learners should use the clues below to complete the crossword puzzle.
Engineering 3: Crossword puzzle with clues for Across and Down.
Teacher’s Copy: Crossword puzzle with answers.

Electricity: One-page Response: Draw a picture of a complete circuit that includes a battery, a light and a switch. Explain how the switch works to control the light. (preventing the flow of electrons to the bulb eliminates the glowing the accompanies electron flow in the filament)

Insulators vs. Conductors: 1. Use examples to explain the typical characteristics of conductors and insulators. (conductors often metallic) 2. Do internet research to investigate and report to your peers on at least three examples of how humans have used insulators as a technology to make life better. (jumper cables, pan handles, screwdriver handles)
Lever: Calculations of Effort (differentiated for stronger learners): Diagram showing Distance from Effort to Fulcrum, Distance from Load to Fulcrum, Beam, Effort, Fulcrum, Load.
Retrieved from: https://www.twinkl.es/teaching-wiki/first-class-lever
In this model, the following relationship can be proven out by experimentation: Effort Distance x Effort Force = Load Distance x Load Force
Have learners fill in the following table (and, if equipment invites, demonstrate their intuition about the numbers using a metre stick, fulcrum, masses and a force spring scale)
Draw a Picture of the Three Classes of Levers. Give an example of each type of lever.
Calculating the Ideal Mechanical Advantage of a Wedge (differentiated calculation for stronger learners)

Wedge IMA: IMA = D_E / D_R, D_E = Distance traveled by the effort = L, D_R = Distance traveled by the resistance = H, IMA = L (⊥ to height) / H. What is the IMA of the wedge on the right? (IMA = 10/3 = 3.33). Learners, what does this number mean? (This means, that ideally, for an effort force of 1 directed down on the wedge you get an output force of 3.33)
Build a Wind-Powered Car. Design retrieved from: MacKinnon, G. R. (2001). Mass production: Not at all beyond elementary school curriculum, Technology and Children 5(2), 3-8.

Design Challenge: The instructions below would invite a teacher/learners to build a model car. It is only meant as a possible starting design. Learners in groups would be expected to create a prototype car which a single learner would race down a hallway by blowing behind the sail. If the hallway is wide enough, several cars could be raced at once. If it is a narrow hallway, the teacher could establish a starting line and a finish line and record the time of travel. To be a true “controlled” experiment to test the speed of the prototypes, the same person should supply the wind power for the trials.
The variables that learners can test for speed by design are: The shape and size of the sail, The size of the cans used for the wheel assembly, The size of the rectangle frame

Beginning design. Materials needed: Car Frame: (8mm x 8mm x18 cm) 4 per car (recommend precut for learners), Cross Member Support for Mast (8 cm x 8cm x 21 cm) drill hole for upright mast, Corner Triangles: (4 cm x 4cm x 5cm) 8 triangles per car frame (recommend heavy stock paper), White or Hot Glue: to affix paper corners on wooden frame, Axle Dowels: (21cm x .64 cm) 2 per car, Mast Dowel: (22cm x 0.5 cm), Soda Can circles (5.5 cm diameter Bristol board)
Rubber tubing (axle stops): (0.64 cm diameter)
Rubber bands: 1 cm width- 6 per car
Sail: standard 8.5 x 11 inch, sheets with holes punched for mast
As car prototypes are compared for speed, learners should make note of the features that promote the fastest designs.
The learners should submit a report that responds to the following rubric: Statement of the design challenge: 2 marks
Sketch with dimensions: 5 marks
Materials list: 3 marks
Recorded data from trials in a neat table (comparing group designs: 5 marks
Discussion of best designs and the rationale for the features that seemed to promote the fastest cars: 5 marks
NOTE: In assembly of prototype wind cars, learners should exercise caution as they use tools to cut wood and bore holes in wood and metal

inclusive_learning_strategies: Human beings are very creative. They are constantly problem solving to make life better. Learners, I am sure you have heard the word technology before. Many people think technology is just computers. Actually, technology has been given a simple definition: “a way of adapting”.
Learners, I want you to look at the following picture and see if you can identify technology. In other words, ways that humans have adapted their world to make life easier.

Teacher Notes: Overhead light (Invention of Electricity), Hinges (Attached by Screws), Door latch (Example of a Lever), Light Switch (Electrical Circuit), Ramp (Example of an Inclined Plane). Let us look at each of these technologies.

Electricity: Often to operate a flashlight or small radio we need a battery. What do we see written on the battery? (some number of volts). Volts are a measure of the potential for the battery to do work. If we take a copper wire and attached it to a battery and a light bulb, we can get the bulb to light but only if it is a closed loop. Below you can see that the first two arrangements would not light a bulb. The closed cycle in the third picture is referred to as an electrical circuit.

But what is happening in the wire? The wire and the tiny filament (wire) in the bulb have something in them called electrons. When we add a battery to a wire in a complete cycle, the battery makes the electrons move which we call electric current. When the electrons move through a thin wire (filament) in a bulb it causes the wire to heat up and glow. This type of current from a battery is called direct current (abbreviated DC). The electricity in our homes is different. It doesn’t come from a battery; it is generated elsewhere and brought into our houses with large wires. It is called alternating current (abbreviated AC). We can still put AC through a filament in a bulb and generate light.

Switches Interrupt Electricity Flow: If you look at the diagram of a direct current circuit below, you see that we can break the cycle by putting in an electric switch. If the switch is opened, the electrons cannot complete the cycle by moving from one end of the battery to the other. Therefore, the filament will not glow and provide light. In our picture of the door above, you can see a light and a switch; both are important technologies (inventions.)
Retrieved from: https://scienceshifu.com/what-are-series-and-parallel-circuits/

Experiments: If the teacher has the materials (wire, battery, light and switch they should have learners build the circuit by hand. If the teacher has access to the internet they should have learners build the circuit below using the PHET simulation at: https://phet.colorado.edu/en/simulations/circuit-construction-kit-dc

Test 1: what happens to the brightness of the bulb if you double the number of batteries in the circuit above? (the light gets brighter). Explain why this happens? (with two batteries you have twice the potential to push electrons through the circuit)

Test 2: What happens to the brightness if you pass the current through two bulbs one after the other? (the bulbs will be dimmer) Why does this happen? (some of the energy from the electron flow is spent in each of the bulbs so that over the light is dimmer)

What Materials Invite Electrons to Flow?: The teacher should set up a demonstration that resembles the diagram below. Questions for learners: If we place a copper wire between Points A & B, we know that the lightbulb will glow from electron flow. We would say that copper is a conductor of electricity. If we place a piece of wood (maybe a ruler) between Points A & B, the lightbulb will remain unlit. We would say that wood acts as an insulator because it does not invite electrons to flow. Let us predict whether the following bridging items will be conductors or insulators and then test them to confirm our predictions.
Material between A & B | Conductor or Insulator? | Aluminium foil | String | Plastic | Rubber eraser | Iron nail | Pencil with exposed graphite ends | Glass | Paper clip

Note: If the teacher has the materials, they may also try water and salt water. Conduction of the salt water can be explained by the fact that NaCl (salt) breaks up into sodium and chloride parts called ions that invite for electrons to flow.

Design Features That Rely on Conductors/Insulators: We use the property of conduction in our technology design if we want electricity to flow. A copper wire often has plastic or rubber coating. The copper wire we want to conduct the electricity but we know it will come in contact with other objects (including our hands) so we put an insulator on the outside so the electricity doesn’t flow beyond the copper wire and give us a shock!

Learners have you ever seen one of these? Retrieved from: https://www.electricalcasy.com/2016/10/insulators-used-in-overhead-power-lines.html. Often on older electrical poles we see glass pieces holding the high voltage wire. The glass acts as an insulator so that the
electricity only goes through the wire and doesn’t jump to other objects.

Door Handles as Levers: In recent years, door handles have been redesigned to make doors easier to open especially by occupants that may have physical challenges grabbing a traditional round doorknob. Learners, have you ever seen somebody move a rock with a piece of wood and a pivot point (triangle in this picture). It might look something like this picture. Retrieved from: https://www.twinkl.es/teaching-wiki/first-class-lever

Why do you suppose they use this combination of objects when they could just lift the rock out of the way? (it might be easier to use this approach). It definitely takes less effort to lift the rock using this simple machine we call a lever. Have you ever heard the word advantage? Can you use it in a sentence? What does it mean in this sentence? Josephine crossed the finished line in the race because she had the advantage of longer legs. (she was well-suited for running). When we use simple machines like the lever, we do so because we are getting a mechanical advantage; it is easier to complete the task with the machine. For instance, humans invented the wheel for things like trolleys and wheelbarrows because it took less effort to move heavy items than simply carrying them by hand. Where else do see the mechanical advantage of a wheel used? (bicycles, cars, prams, pianos, medical stretchers)

Problem With Levers: Which arrangement of a lever do you think would take the least effort to lift the stone (load) by applying an effort force downward at the red arrow? Why? Our intuition is that the second lever arrangement would require the least effort force downward. Try it with a ruler, eraser and book! Retrieved from: https://letstalkscience.ca/educational-resources/backgrounders/simple-machines-levers

This is one type of lever called a first-class lever. In a first-class lever, the fulcrum is located between the load and the effort. When the fulcrum is closer to the load, then less effort is needed to move the load.

Other types of levers help us solve practical problems. Classes of Levers: [Table showing Class 1, Class 2, Class 3 levers with diagrams and descriptions]. Retrieved from: https://worksheetzone.org/the-three-classes-of-levers-

More Simple Machines That Give us Mechanical Advantage: INCLINED PLANE: A tilted surface, or slope, is also known as an inclined plane. It is a basic machine for raising loads. The simplest form is the ramp, which the ancient Egyptians are believed to have used for building the pyramids. If workers tried to lift a block of stone straight up, they would strain their muscles. By dragging the block up a ramp they had to move the block further, but the effort was reduced, making it easier. Other machines that use inclined planes include the screw and the wedge. Retrieved from: https://steemit.com/technology/@ghostgtr/how-stuff-works-machines-and-energy

The inclined plane is a simple machine that has helped humans a very long time. A good example is the building of the pyramids in Egypt. These structures were made of large stones and it was near impossible for the workers to lift the stones but...they had the necessary effort force to slide it on a ramp that we call an inclined plane.
They also would place logs under the stone to help it roll up the plane. This demonstrates human problem solving in that the logs were an early version of the wheel, yet another simple machine.
What re the parts of a wheel? (axle, spokes, circumference)
How is the mechanical advantage of wheels used for devices in our world today? (wheelbarrow, carts, cars, bicycles, pulleys, water wheels)
A video resource to teach about the wheel and axle if the teacher has access to technology.

The teacher can pose the following questions to learners before they watch the video: a) what are 5 examples of the wheel and axle in the video. (well wheel, steering wheel car and boat, hand drill, rolling pin, bike pedal) b) How does the wheel and axle apply mechanical advantage? (larger wheel with less effort turns smaller wheel many times)

Measuring Forces With A Spring Balance: Let us see the difference in effort force by using a force meter (called a spring balance) to measure the force necessary to: a) Lift a mass (marbles in a bag) directly upward against gravity. What is the force measure? b) Pull the same bag of marbles up a ramp. What is the force measure?
It should look like this: [Image of spring balance pulling marbles up a ramp]
You may pull the marbles a longer distance than a direct lift but, you will find that the effort force is less. The inclined plane gives us a mechanical advantage.

Calculating the Ideal Mechanical Advantage.(IMA): Inclined Plane IMA IMA = D_E / D_R, D_E = Distance traveled by the effort = L, D_R = Distance traveled by the resistance = H, IMA = L (slope) / H. What is the IMA of the inclined plane above?

I M A = 15/4 = 3.75. This is saying that ideally using the ramp to lift this car is almost 4 time easier than lifting the cart from the floor to the top of the ramp. But if you use a spring balance to actually measure the ratio of the Force to lift the cart versus the Force to drag the cart up the plane you will get a number for mechanical advantage smaller than 3.75 (maybe 3.25) in other words, the Actual Mechanical Advantage A M A). The AMA is always smaller than the IMA! Learners, why do you think we don’t get quite as much mechanical advantage as we thought? (friction between the load and the surface of the ramp). In our picture at the beginning of class we see a ramp leading up to the door. Particularly for a learner in a wheelchair, the effort force to cruise up the ramp is less than lifting the chair from the ground to the door sill, something that would not be possible for a person confined to a wheelchair.

More Examples of Inclined Planes: Wedge& Screw. The shape of an inclined plane is special. If we turn it to face the point down, it resembles another important simple machine called a wedge. Learners, can you tell me some examples of where we use a wedge? (door-stop, closing up cracks in wood floors, splitting wood).

Retrieved from: https://inventorsoftomorrow.com/2016/10/12/wedges-2/. If you look closely at a screw, you will see that it is really just an inclined plane wrapped around a centre cylinder. The example below is a paper inclined plane wrapped around a pencil.

Retrieved from: https://www.geocities.ws/jaywilliams.geo/simmach.html. Learners, what problem does the screw solve for us; how are they used? (fasteners for wood and metal). Did you notice the hinge on the door in our first picture? It is a very useful technology for inviting a door to swing open and closed but…it needs to be fastened to the door and the casing surrounding the door in order to operate. That is where a screw is useful. Keep in mind, that as the screw is inserted into wood, it exerts great force on the wood. How do you suppose a screw is used to fasten a hinge on a door? (we use a screwdriver as a tool to insert the screw). Screws have different head impressions, so we have to choose the correct screwdriver. Can you tell me the shape on the screw heads you have observed?

In early days carpenters only had access to slot screws. Why did humans invent more head designs? (the slot screwdriver would slip out of the slot when great twisting motion (torque) was applied. The new heads reduced the slippage.) So, to place a hinge on the door, we would: select wood screws of a length that could hold our heavy door. choose a good screw head design to reduce slippage. keep the wood from splitting, by marking the screw holes in the hinge (while it was held to the door in the right position) and drilling small holes where the screws will be inserted. Turn the screw in a clockwise motion until it pressed the hinge hard against the door. Nuts and bolts are also useful fasteners that make use of a spiral thread like the screw. We can fasten items together by holding the bolt tightly with a wrench and turning the nut clockwise to tighten, counterclockwise to loosen.

Technological Designs That Enhance Structural Support
You will notice in our initial door picture that the inclined plane has supporting structures that serve to strengthen the ramp. If those supports were not in place, what might happen to the ramp? (it could collapse under stress)
Humans have developed many supporting structures/inventions to resist the stress that large forces put on the object.
Learner investigation on the internet
Identify and describe how forces can alter shapes of objects. E.g. bending, stretching, pulling, twisting
Human beings have done much work to create stronger structures. A bridge for example or geodesic dome use triangles to improve strength.
Geodesic Domes are particularly strong. They use to be used for enclosing radar for tracking airplanes at airports.

Scaffolding for building construction is another example of how stress forces can be reduced by structural ties.
Ties Are Used to Support Housing Structures
Depending on the force that is placed on a roof, collar ties and rafter ties help to maintain the structure from collapsing.

Struts to Reduce Compression
Struts are used in airplanes to strengthen the wheel assembly against violet bumps as the airplane lands.
Struts are used in automobiles to reduce abrupt bumps in the road.
Ask learners where they have seen struts used? (motorcycles, jet airplanes, bicycles, ATVs, minivan rear window supports)
